payment system (PPS), the labor-related portion of the payment rates is adjusted for differences in wages and benefits across areas. This adjustment is made using a wage index defined for designated urban and rural labor markets. The Health Care Financing Administration (HCFA) defines an entire metropolitan statistical area (MSA) to be a single urban labor market. For rural hospitals, all rural counties in a single State are defined to be competing in one labor market. For each of these urban and labor markets, an areawide weighted average wage per hour is calculated and converted into an index for which the national weighted average is 1.0. Executives of rural hospitals located near urban areas claim that their wages are higher than those in rural hospitals not adjacent to an MSA. The reason given is that workers living near the MSA can commute to an urban hospital and receive higher urban wages. The adjacent rural hospitals must consequently pay wages that are competitive with the higher urban wages. The executives argue that their hospitals should be classified in a way that reflects this wage competition, suggesting that they should not be grouped with more remote rural hospitals for the purpose of calculating the wage index. They propose that counties adjacent to MSAs should have their own separate labor market designation or be included within the nearby urban area's labor market. This would lead to a higher wage index value and, consequently, increased Medicare reimbursements for these hospitals, better reflecting the actual cost of labor in their locations. HCFA periodically reviews and updates its policies based on data analysis and feedback from healthcare providers. The agency may consider conducting studies or pilot programs to assess the validity of these claims and determine if adjustments are needed in how wage indices are calculated for rural hospitals near urban areas, ensuring fairness and accuracy

e note that this should only be used as a part of another text rather than standing alone: Bank A can claim against property with priority over Bank B if the second advance taken from it is tacked to an initial mortgage given at first loan advancement, otherwise; claims made by bank b will have higher precedence regarding sums lent. But it will be able to claim against the property in priority to Bank B with respect to its second advance only if it is permitted to tack the second advance to the mortgage that was taken at the time the first advance was made. If Bank A is not permitted to tack the second advance, then Bank B's claim in respect of the sums that it lent will have priority over Bank A's claims with respect to the second advance.
